camera_defaults.angle: low position with restrained upward angle
camera_defaults.lens: medium telephoto or telephoto compression unless confined-space proximity requires wide angle
camera_defaults.depth: medium depth or deep focus when scale relation must remain readable

decisive_pressure_frame.use_when: pressure 4-5 or when the brief asks for extreme oppression, awe, dread, or a decisive cinematic first read
composition_cues: dark mass crossing the upper frame
one readable head, claw, eye, hand, rib arc, shadow, or body segment
lighting_cues: backlight or lightning behind the dominant fragment
low-key exposure with readable edge texture
foreground practical lights kept small against the mass
guardrails: avoid replacing witness scale with poster display
avoid pushing the decisive fragment to the far edge
keep at least one foreground witness or instrument anchor
keep the colossus partially hidden or frame-overflowing
do not add secondary detail that weakens the first-read threat

director_shot_spine.use_when: when the image has enough pressure but lacks a directorly one-frame intention
practical_applications: name where the first look lands
route the eye from foreground witness or frame edge to the subject fragment
explain why the subject is visible in this instant
use roof, window, cave, cloud, water, dust, or shadow edges as framing evidence
demote rain, lights, crowds, debris, and texture when they compete with the primary image
guardrails: do not let checklist detail compete with the primary image
keep the primary focus readable through reveal light
do not remove witness scale to make a clean poster
do not add a shot list; keep it one decisive frame
do not copy external skill wording

director_subtraction_pass.use_when: pressure 4-5, or when the frame has pressure and scale but still reads like several small actions instead of one major event
subtractive_checks: if two actions read equally strong, demote one into reaction, silhouette, or atmosphere
if a bright effect reads before the subject, shrink it, darken it, or move it behind the event hierarchy
if foreground weapons, debris, branches, beams, or architecture cut the frame into pieces, keep only the ones that route the eye
if destruction does not read as the result of the main force, merge it into dust, smoke, shadow, or aftermath
guardrails: do not remove witness scale or environment scale while simplifying
do not solve clutter by collapsing the scene into a close hero portrait
do not sterilize weather, fracture, or atmosphere to create fake cleanliness
do not let secondary reaction beats become equal-strength micro-events

controlled_fracture_coherence.use_when: when weather, damage, occlusion, darkness, or broken surfaces create scale and pressure but risk becoming visual mess
allowed_chaos_materials: rain streaks, haze, debris, partial occlusion, broken reflections, damaged surfaces
mud, soot, scratches, condensation, water marks, dust, silt, torn fabric, bent trees, snapped flags
fragmented light beams, imperfect lens edges, frame-cropped architecture, damaged vehicles or structures
coherence_rules: every chaotic element must point to focus, scale, motion, or atmosphere
keep directional forces consistent across rain, fabric, trees, smoke, dust, and reflections
separate readable subject silhouette from messy texture
keep foreground clutter subordinate to the viewer-eye path
guardrails: do not sterilize the scene
do not let texture become random clutter
do not let brokenness hide the primary subject
do not replace controlled weather and damage with clean decorative polish

shot_economy_mode.use_when: pressure 4-5, or whenever the prompt is becoming correct but visually crowded
texture_budget_policy: texture exists only when it supports the primary image or one supporting vector
foreground clutter must either lead the eye or be darkened, blurred, cropped, or removed
background detail must either show scale or dissolve into atmosphere
demotion_policy: keep secondary action as reaction or aftermath when the main event is already readable
turn extra figures into silhouettes when they compete with the subject
turn extra debris into directional streaks or atmosphere when it competes with the subject
turn extra lights into small practical points when they compete with reveal light
negative_space_policy: preserve quiet or dark zones when they strengthen the primary image
allow low-detail atmosphere when it improves scale and pressure
do not treat every empty region as a defect
guardrails: do not increase pressure by filling every region
do not satisfy every profile surface at equal visual weight
do not remove human-scale or instrument-scale anchors
do not collapse the scale vector into a close character shot
do not make the scene sterile while reducing clutter
